segments.title: Haida and the Tribal-class ships that were not preserved
segments.summary: A first view of HMCS Haida leads into a reflection on the distinguished Tribal-class destroyers lost in war or sent to the breakers.
segments.body: Standing beside Haida for the first time, Dr. Clarke explains why her survival matters while stressing that several other Tribals also merited preservation. He names Nubian for her battle honours, Eskimo for surviving the loss of her bow, and Tartar as another highly regarded ship. The moment connects the emotional force of seeing a preserved warship with the much larger part of the class that survives only in records and memory.

evidence.note: Dr. Clarke sees Haida, then discusses Nubian, Eskimo, Tartar, and the loss of the other ships to war or scrapping.

segments.title: Why ocean-going destroyers had to be large
segments.summary: Haida's physical scale illustrates why destroyers serving fast fleets across the Atlantic and Pacific could not be truly small ships.
segments.body: The ship's size provides a direct correction to the familiar description of destroyers as the navy's little ships. Dr. Clarke argues that Atlantic and Pacific distances, together with the need to operate alongside fast carriers and capital ships, demanded a substantial hull. Haida's scale is therefore not excess for its own sake but a visible consequence of range, seaworthiness, speed, and fleet-screening duties.

evidence.note: He contrasts the destroyer's imposing size with the little-ships label and ties that size to ocean operations with fast fleet units.

segments.title: Tribal-class forward firepower in destroyer combat
segments.summary: The Tribal class combined a threatening head-on silhouette with rapid forward gunfire intended to overpower enemy destroyers and light cruisers.
segments.body: Using Haida's appearance as a starting point, Dr. Clarke asks the viewer to imagine several British destroyers approaching in darkness. Their forward gun concentration let them keep a narrow aspect while bringing a high rate of fire to bear, creating an advantage over an opponent forced to choose between presenting a broadside or accepting inferior head-on firepower. He links this design purpose to destroyer fighting at Narvik and to action against Italian light forces at Cape Bon.

evidence.note: He describes the Tribals' forward guns, rate-of-fire advantage, and intended role overwhelming destroyers and light cruisers.

segments.title: Tribal-class losses and wartime mission overload
segments.summary: Heavy Tribal-class losses are connected to a fleet-wide shortage that pushed the ships into too many demanding missions.
segments.body: Dr. Clarke notes the severe wartime attrition among the 27 Tribal-class destroyers, including 14 of the 16 Royal Navy ships and one Canadian ship. He does not present those losses simply as a verdict on the design. Instead, he emphasizes that the ships were repeatedly committed because the fleet lacked enough destroyers and cruisers to cover all of its tasks, making their fate part of a broader force-availability problem.

evidence.note: The transcript gives the class loss figures and attributes their demanding employment to shortages of destroyers and cruisers.
